just want to set the stage.

think down the road, if we feel like there's a world where we want to go straight to publish right away, we can always revisit that.

I think it's just helpful to kind of set the stage to really summarize on a high level why we're here and what I've heard so far as, like...
The big issues are, in the past, you optimized for traffic volume over pipeline.
Your content over-indexed on IT security practitioners and not decision makers.
And you also have some really, you have some content gaps in key topic areas that you would like to build authority in.
And you also don't have a lot, you don't have any comparison content with your top competitors.
So this is, these are kind of the challenges that we're coming up against that we need to solve for you.

And we're also dealing with a decrease in organic search traffic that we also need to turn around with content that's actually ranking in Google and attracting visitors to your website, as well as laying the foundation for that content to also appear in AI responses in different LLMs.
that's, that's, my the goals of

Obviously, are to generate pipeline from organic search traffic, but also to build topical authority that supports your new two-pillar positioning, identity security and data security, all with the purpose of drawing awareness to the one secure platform.
So, before we build any client strategy, we like to dive into the existing content analysis.
You've already given us some great insights around what the content production has been so far.
We understand that you have a glossary part of your website and the blog part of your website.
The glossary is much thinner, and the content there focuses mostly on cyber attacks, compliant frameworks, and it's very definitional.
So, what is content?
And it's not super long.
So, I think the average length is around 500 words based on just a couple of entries that I've reviewed.
For the blog content, obviously, the PowerShell tutorials that you've already pointed out, they are the largest cluster.
But it's my understanding that in recent months, you've also begun expanding into other topic clusters to kind of broaden your authority in different areas.
And for those, we will be continuing to build on the work you've done so far.
I do have to say, I understand that you would like to also do content refreshes, and it is a priority whenever we are working with a client with a huge content library.
We also want to boost the existing content and make it work even harder.
I understand that you've done a content pruning exercise a couple of months ago, which is great for SEO.
So, as part of the sprint, we will also do a content audit to identify what are the top refresh opportunities that we should target as part of phase two.
It's just that in phase one, we really want to make...
Make sure that we nail the voice and the tone and the product messaging in new content.
And then for refreshes, we can apply those same learnings.

Like, it's not our sweet spot.
mean, it's kind of a, it attracts the audience, which I guess is good, but it's just like some of these other topics.

do have to highlight that we pulled a list of URLs from SEMrush.

Thank you.
We also do a competitor content analysis.
I understand you have a lot of different competitors, but these are the ones that we plucked out based on the names that you brought up during the kickoff call.
And we really, we analyze, we always analyze competitors' content because we want to understand what's working for them, what can we steal.
And where do we need to be more visible in?
And obviously, companies like SentinelOne and ManageEngine, they have huge content libraries.

SentinelOne, for example, they have a whole subfolder on their website called Cybersecurity One-on-One, where they publish not just a finitional what is content, but they also publish listicles like product comparisons.
They publish middle of the funnel content.
It's their SEO hub, really.
And what we found is that a lot of companies, you know, the company...

that we're working with, they do have a dedicated section of their website that's just dedicated to like SEO, informational content that's there to kind of grow traffic and funnel that traffic to the right pages.

Again, you can kind of click through and see what different competitors are covering, but the key insights that we were able to uncover in the topic clusters they're covering, we have used them to develop the topic clusters for networks.
So the key thing to know about how we approach content strategies is we build topic clusters, and this is a very tried and true SEO approach.

We pick lanes where we want to build authority, and that allows us to avoid a spray and pray approach where we're just publishing content at random, helps us avoid publishing content that's not connected to your audience or your product and has like no value.

And it also allows us to measure search and LLM performance more effectively because when we build your looker dashboard, we can see, okay, what are the top clusters that are driving the most traffic to your site and then we can also use that to inform additional content research.
Like I said, because you already have an existing content library, refreshes will also be a part of the long-term content strategy.
The main thing to know for these clusters is that they were informed by the two pillars that you are prioritizing right now, data security and identity security.
So the first cluster that we want to target, I mean, this is not an order of priority, but this one is certainly a priority, which is DSPM.
It will include both foundational content.
For example, we have some example keywords here like data security.
Posture Management, you're ranking for it, but your landing page is ranking for it on page two.
You have a blog about DSPM trends that's ranking, I think, on page six or seven.
So we want to make sure that we are covering this topic from every possible angle.

So both informational content that is still authoritative, speaks to decision makers, but we also cover the more, I want to say, like bottom of the funnel or just like more intricate angles that might have lower search volume but are still very valuable to help you build authority in this space because this is a big priority.
Each cluster contains an explanation of what it would cover, the potential topics, the personas, and why we're recommending it so you can kind of understand what's our rationale.
Here, obviously, it's a clear priority, and the only competitor that we've analyzed so far with meaningful DSPM content is here.
For the second one, the second one would focus on identity, security, and threat detection.

So this would be focusing on the other pillar, and it would also include foundational IAM concepts, but it would also cover emerging areas like ITDR, machine and ephemeral identities, and zero standing privileges.
I think here we could really bring up some things that Chanel mentioned around like AI and machine identities that a lot of your prospects are really excited about.
So again, here we could make sure we plug any content gaps that you have right now in the more foundational content.
But then we could also find one of my favorite things is to discover keywords that are clearly emerging, and nobody's caught on to them yet.
And they're usually very low volume, but as interest spikes, so does the search volume.
And that allows us to kind of be one of the first, it allows you to be one of the first companies to talk about something.
It really does build your authority over time.
So that would be the plan for the second cluster.
The third one would focus on Microsoft security gaps.
This is something that you already have.

Some of your existing content already covers this topic cluster, but we would make sure to go a little bit deeper and to explore specific use cases around Microsoft security gaps that might not have a lot of volume.
But really do target the right audience and bring the right traffic to your site.
For example, some of the example keywords we could target are, is Microsoft Copilot secure?
Does Copilot send data to Microsoft?
SharePoint sprawl, Teams sprawl.
So different types of issues with Microsoft Copilot security and Microsoft 365 security.
So you mentioned that even though it doesn't, these.
Gap fillers don't necessarily give us a lot of traffic.
It's more, you said something about it might not bring as much traffic, but is the traffic higher quality or do we not know that?
Yeah, the traffic is higher quality because somebody who is Googling this co-pilot send data to Microsoft, there's a higher likelihood.
And we also analyze the search results.
And it's very clear that the people who are Googling this are people that are working for businesses who are concerned about this.
So it's very important for us to find, we don't want to target consumer keywords or, you know, just security topics that individuals are interested in.
But I do want to emphasize that this strategy will target both higher volume topics to make sure you're recovering the search traffic.
But we will also balance that with lower volume topics that are higher intent, meaning that it's a very, it's a...
We're targeting a specific subset of your audience, if you understand what I mean.
So Azure Active Directory, for example, it has 14,000 searches per month.
Some of these are just not going to be relevant for you, but we will also complement that with more higher intent keywords.
Okay.
And then quick question for you.
terms of the, I appreciate the comment about not wanting to spray and pray good.
How do we think about with the limit of number of articles that we're going to be getting from GrowthX on a given time period, how we're going to prioritize?
Like, is it going to be that we're going to really attack one cluster first?
Is it that we're going to diversify across several clusters and do like one here, one here, one here?
How does that generally work best when you're getting a new client running?
Yeah.
So that's a great question.
So we do five articles per week for, during the sprint, we.
We like to cover a range of clusters just because it gives us an opportunity to get feedback on different types of topics.
So we want to do commercial keywords where we're talking about your platform, your competitors.
We want to do basically a little bit of everything, which I know sounds bad, but it does help us get more feedback from you so we can calibrate.

Once we, I would say like around week five or six, once we deliver you a couple of like maybe five to eight pieces of content, that's when I can put together a plan and say, okay, this is what we prioritize targeting.
This is where we want to put our focus for the next maybe eight weeks.
This is where we want to build the authority.
And then we can kind of decide, okay, this is the priority cluster.
And typically we do like to combine very commercial keywords with more educational keywords.
There's a cluster here that's just commercial keywords.
So that one's going to be a priority always, but we want to.
Also make sure that we have an educational angle as well, or like an informational one.
Okay.
And then a follow-up question to that.
I appreciate the sprint is kind of its own thing.

Has there been a situation, I would hope so, where you've had a client host Sprint, where they're still engaged with GrowthX, where they're like, hey, we're seeing the results are good, things are going in the right direction.
We want to accelerate.
so instead of five, want to talk about 10 articles a week.
And what does that look like?
I appreciate most likely we'd have to do an amendment to what we have.

We definitely have the capacity.
We can scale in different ways.
So we can scale both in terms of articles, but we can also...
So we have a team of directors who are SEO and SEO experts, and they can also build a programmatic SEO strategy for you.
I don't know if you've discussed this with anybody before, but programmatic SEO might mean like template pages or tool pages.
Basically, we could be publishing like 10 to 20 pages per month that are easy to create from a content standpoint.
So, for example, one example would be like a CVE directory that could be created programmatically.
Or just, you know, we would have to brainstorm the angle, obviously, but that's also a different angle that we could take to like scale even more.
So you could be publishing like 10 or 20 pages per week, but it wouldn't be on the blog.
It would be its own thing.
Understood.
Okay, thank you.
Quick one on my end.

I assume that we might be willing to create product pages or feature pages instead.

And we can have my team work on the appropriate content.
I just feel like this team on this call is in a better position to make that final decision.
Yeah, that makes sense.
And we will obviously need to pass over the list of keywords to our SEO team just to avoid our labs because they might be cooking something on their end, too.
In the interest of time, I can't provide some thoughts here, but I can write a message later over Slack.
So when it comes to product pages versus blogs, they typically don't rank for the same keywords.
And even if they do, sometimes you actually want to have both because your product page might be ranking super high on Google search.
But a blog post has more context and it could appear in LLM responses.
And we...
we...
we...
I have the opportunity to provide way more information in a blog post, and that's why it's valuable to have both.
But I can follow up later over Slack as well.
Okay, so the fourth cluster would focus around different security use cases and scenarios.

We selected this cluster based on the fact that this is something that you brought up, that you've seen some success when it comes to organic traffic, where people come with a specific pain point or a use case, then they discover a solution on your site, and then they kind of progress and they become a client later on.

And this is also a tried and tested SEO approach where we find specific pain points or issues or challenges or interests that different parts of your audience could be having, and then we create content around those topics, and then we naturally present you as the solution.
So, for example, this could include content around how to detect insider threats.
threats, how to prepare for a security audit, how to prepare for a ransomware attack.

You already have some content around ransomware attacks, but it focuses on prevention, but none of them focus on preparation, which I think is also an important angle considering ransomware attacks seem to be inevitable these days.
So I think it's a useful angle to have.
The fifth one, very straightforward.
This is the cluster that would have all of the competitive differentiation content and alternatives.
So this would have all of your versus blog posts, best tool, listicles, comparisons, alternatives.
One of the, you can find some of the example keywords here.
Naturally, they're very low volume, but the traffic here is incredibly valuable.
And they're, these types of blog posts are so important for SEO and GEO.
For the last couple of clients that I've been.
Working with, we will publish a comparison blog post, comparing them and a competitor or two competitors.
They might not even be talking about themselves that much in the blog post.
And it will be picked up by perplexity, by AI overviews, by Google Gemini within a couple of weeks, and it will be cited.

So it's really giving us the opportunity to teach LLM how to talk about Netwrix and make sure that when somebody asks them about you or ask them about alternatives to Varonis or other competitors, that it has the right information about your company.
And then this cluster would focus on hybrid and on-premises security.
So this was informed by the fact that Varonis has discontinued on-prem support.

So we want to make sure that we really have authority in this space and we can educate both the readers and LLMs that Netwrix is a solution that is flexible and can support different types of companies regardless of...
We also have some example keywords here as well.
So those would be the six keywords.

It seems reasonable to me.

And maybe if there's some data, or if we sort of see that this is ranking somehow importantly, then great.
I'm happy to be on.
I just don't know.

Scan data in both environments.

Seems, yeah, seems really broad.
I mean, I think it's fine.
Like, don't know how big you need the clusters to be.
But knowing that we have identity security and data security, you know, it could cover a lot of different personas.
I see that you have the personas down there.

It's just a consideration of whether it might be too broad.

different like security incidents or just use cases so we can build that in.
